author: Dr. Lizard
title: Why does FLAER bind to PNH cells differently than normal cells?
answer: FLAER is a fluorescent bacterial toxin that specifically binds GPI anchors, which are absent on PNH cells.
detail: FLAER stands for fluorescent aerolysin, a modified bacterial toxin from Aeromonas hydrophila that binds GPI anchors directly.
PNH cells lack GPI anchors due to PIGA mutations, so they show dim or absent FLAER binding.
FLAER is more sensitive than CD55 or CD59 antibodies, detecting clones as small as 0.01 percent.
FLAER is not used on red blood cells because glycophorin causes nonspecific weak binding that reduces accuracy.
releaseNote: FLAER binds to PNH cells differently than normal cells due to the absence of GPI anchors, which FLAER specifically targets.
